summary: A biographical and philosophical overview of David Hume, detailing his empiricist approach to human nature, his skeptical critique of causality, and his sentiment-based theory of morality.

principles.statement: All ideas are derived from impressions.
principles.rationale: The mind does not create ideas out of nothing; it only compounds, transposes, augments, or diminishes the original data of sensation or internal consciousness.
principles.source_quote: the mind does not create any ideas but derives them from impressions.

principles.statement: Causality is a habitual association, not an observable necessary connection.
principles.rationale: We cannot observe a physical or logical causal link between events, only that they frequently and uniformly go together, which creates a psychological habit or feeling of compulsion.
principles.source_quote: when people regard any events as causally connected, all that they do and can observe is that they frequently and uniformly go together.

principles.statement: Morality is grounded in sentiment and sympathy, not reason.
principles.rationale: Moral goodness is determined by feelings of approval or disapproval regarding the utility or agreeableness of behaviors to oneself or others.
principles.source_quote: Moral decisions are grounded in moral sentiment.

principles.statement: Reason is subordinate to the passions.
principles.rationale: Human behavior and moral choices are driven by affective, emotional orders, with reason playing only a secondary, instrumental role.
principles.source_quote: reason is...the slave of the passions

frameworks.name: Twofold Classification of Meaning (Hume's Fork)
frameworks.when_to_apply: When evaluating whether a statement or concept contains demonstrative knowledge or merely describes matters of fact.
steps: Determine if the concept is a 'relation of ideas' (e.g., math, geometry) which can be known by rational inspection.
Determine if the concept is a 'matter of fact' (e.g., empirical observations) which reveals no logical relations and could conceivably be otherwise.
Accept that only relations of ideas offer demonstrative knowledge, while matters of fact must be accepted exactly as they are given.

mental_models.name: Impressions vs. Ideas
mental_models.description: A lens for understanding human awareness. Impressions are vivid, original data of sensation or consciousness. Ideas are less vivid copies derived from compounding or diminishing those impressions.
mental_models.example: A word only has linguistic meaning if it brings to mind an object that can be traced back to an original impression.

mental_models.name: Belief as Vividness
mental_models.description: Belief is not a separate logical category but a psychological state—a 'liveliness or vividness' that accompanies an idea, usually transferred from an associated impression.
mental_models.example: When people see a glass fall (an impression), the liveliness of that impression infects the associated idea of it breaking, creating the belief that it will break.

heuristics: Trace every idea back to its original impression to determine if it has valid meaning.
Judge moral qualities by their utility or agreeableness to their owners or to others.

signature_quotes.text: reason is...the slave of the passions
signature_quotes.context: Describing the subordinate role of reason in human behavior and motivation.

signature_quotes.text: it fell dead-born from the press, without reaching such distinction, as even to excite a murmur among the zealots.
signature_quotes.context: Hume describing the poor initial reception of his highly ambitious first work, 'A Treatise of Human Nature'.

anti_patterns.description: Assuming causality is a logically demonstrable property of objects themselves.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: We can only observe that events frequently and uniformly go together, not a 'necessary connexion' between them. The contradictory of every matter of fact is always conceivable.

anti_patterns.description: Believing that miracles can be proven by evidence.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Hume notoriously denied that a miracle can be proved by any amount or kind of evidence, as it contradicts the uniform experience of natural laws.
